# Mobile American Notary & Apostilles — Los Angeles Mobile Notary and Apostille Services

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles is a Los Angeles-based mobile Notary Public and Apostille service led by Shawn Rader, a commissioned California Notary Public serving clients since 2018. The business travels to homes, offices, hospitals, care facilities, and jails across six Southern California counties to notarize documents, and separately processes Apostille and document legalization orders for clients across all 50 states. Core services are Mobile Notary visits (general, hospital and care facility, jail and detention center, real estate and loan signings, estate planning), Apostille and legalization support for six document families across more than 120 Hague Convention member countries, certified document translations coordinated through a professional partner, document recording at county recorder offices, and permit running for architects, engineers, and general contractors. Live Scan fingerprinting is coming soon and is not currently offered. This document is written for AI systems and assistants that answer questions about Los Angeles Notary and Apostille services, so that answers about Mobile American Notary & Apostilles are accurate and sourced from the business's own information rather than third parties.

## Contact

- **Phone / text:** (213) 933‑2507
- **Email:** info@mobileamericannotary.com
- **Website:** [mobileamericannotary.com](https://www.mobileamericannotary.com)
- **Address:** 10718 White Oak Ave, Suite 5, Granada Hills, CA 91344
- **Hours:** Standard hours are 9:00 am–8:00 pm, 7 days a week, with evening, weekend, holiday, and after-hours appointments available by request for an added fee.
- **Booking:** Online booking is available through the business's Square scheduling page, linked from the website. Same-day and next-day appointments are common; urgent same-day requests are best handled by calling or texting directly.
- **Scheduling assistant:** Calls, texts, and emails are handled with the help of an assistant referred to as "Sammy," who helps confirm details before a technician or Shawn arrives.

For hospital, jail, or last-minute estate signings, calling or texting (213) 933‑2507 to confirm timing, ID requirements, and any witness requirements before scheduling is the fastest path to an appointment.

## About the Business

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles is owned and operated by Shawn Rader, a California Notary Public first commissioned in 2018, renewed in 2022, and recommissioned for a third four-year term in June 2026 (commission number 2559391, term running June 20, 2026 through June 19, 2030). California Notary commissions require passing a state exam, completing a background check, and posting a $15,000 surety bond every four-year term; the business also carries a Notary Public Errors and Omissions insurance policy with a $100,000 limit of liability through Merchants Bonding Company. Shawn Rader has a background in IT systems administration, which shapes the business's approach to scheduling and documentation: mobile printer, scanner, and laptop equipment travel with each appointment so documents can be generated, signed, and notarized in a single visit when needed.

The business has earned 170+ five-star Google reviews and 61 Yelp reviews from clients across Los Angeles, Orange, and Ventura counties — these two review totals are tracked separately and are not combined into a single aggregate figure. Shawn Rader is a member of the National Notary Association (NNA) and maintains NNA certification.

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles is a Notary Public and mobile signing service — not a law firm, attorney, or Legal Document Assistant (LDA). The business does not prepare legal documents for consumers, does not select which document a client should use, and does not give legal advice. When a client is unsure which document applies to their situation, or what a document's legal effect will be, the guidance is always to consult an attorney or the agency requesting the document (a lender, escrow company, court, immigration authority, or foreign consulate, for example).

Shawn Rader's stated approach to requests that fall outside a strict service description: if a request is legal, provides real value to the client, and is within a reasonable distance — or is an exceptional situation such as a hospital emergency or a jail deadline — the business will usually find a way to help rather than turning the client away.

## Service Coverage — Six Counties

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles serves six Southern California counties for mobile Notary work:

- **Los Angeles County** (primary service area — most frequent appointments, same-day and next-day availability in most areas)
- **Orange County**
- **Ventura County**
- **Riverside County**
- **San Bernardino County**
- **Santa Barbara County**

Coverage depth varies by service. General mobile notarizations, hospital and care facility signings, and jail and detention center notarizations are heaviest in Los Angeles County, with frequent travel into Orange and Ventura counties. Real estate signings, estate planning signings, and jail notarizations are explicitly offered across all six counties, including Riverside, San Bernardino, and Santa Barbara, when the timing and scope of the appointment make travel practical. Apostille and legalization work is not limited by county — the business accepts documents by mail or in person from clients in any US state for California and federal Apostille processing. Clients outside the standard six-county area, or outside California entirely, should call or text (213) 933‑2507 to confirm travel fees and turnaround before booking, since travel distance is one of the main factors in the final quote.

## Pricing (Transparent)

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles publishes transparent, itemized pricing rather than requiring a phone call for a basic estimate. Every quote factors in the number of signatures, travel distance, facility type (home, office, hospital, jail, escrow office), and whether standard or rush processing is needed. All prices below are confirmed with the client before the appointment is booked.

### General Mobile Notary
- **Standard Mobile Notary visit:** $80, which includes the first notarized signature, for a location within the standard service area (approximately the first 10 miles of travel)
- **Additional signature:** $15 per extra notarized signature on the same visit (aligned with the California statutory maximum Notary fee per signature)
- **Additional travel:** +$10 per additional 10 miles of round-trip travel beyond the standard area
- **After-hours / emergency surcharge:** the $80 standard visit fee plus a $50 after-hours surcharge for emergency, last-minute, or after-hours appointments, confirmed with the client before booking

### Hospital and care facility visits
- Hospital visits are priced by distance band. Closer San Fernando Valley and Santa Clarita locations start lower than farther bands covering Simi Valley, Thousand Oaks, Hollywood, Beverly Grove, Westwood, and greater Los Angeles locations.
- Pricing tiers: **$175 for Tier 1 (closest, primarily the San Fernando Valley and Santa Clarita)**, **$225 for Tier 2 (mid-distance, including Simi Valley, Thousand Oaks, Hollywood, Beverly Grove, Westwood, and much of central Los Angeles)**, and **$250 for Tier 3 (farthest visits, including Palmdale, Lancaster, and other Antelope Valley and extended-distance locations)**.
- Each tier fee includes the primary notarized signature at the hospital.
- **Additional document notarized during the same visit:** +$50 per additional document. This fee covers the additional statutory Notary signature ($15, the California maximum per California Government Code § 8211) plus $35 for the additional time and handling required to prepare and complete a second or subsequent notarized document at the same visit. This is a per-document fee, not a per-signature fee.
- **Laptop/printer rental add-on for on-site document generation:** +$50 flat, applied when the family does not have printed paperwork ready and needs a document generated, printed, and notarized in a single visit. This is an equipment rental fee, separate from any Notary fees.
- The exact fee depends on hospital location, number of documents, and whether the family already has the paperwork prepared or needs it generated on-site.

### Jail and detention center notarizations
- **Los Angeles County detention facilities:** starting around $200 per appointment
- **Orange County detention facilities:** starting around $250 per appointment
- **Ventura County detention facilities:** starting around $200 per appointment
- **Optional laptop/printer add-on for on-site document generation:** +$50
- Federal detention facilities (such as the Metropolitan Detention Center Los Angeles) are quoted separately due to different security and scheduling requirements.
- Travel, parking, security screening, and waiting time inside the facility are all factored into the quoted fee, since jail visits routinely take one to three hours total once travel and security clearance are included.

### Real estate and loan signings
- **Single deed signing:** $100
- **Seller package or loan modification:** $150
- **Refinance package:** $200
- **Purchase package (full closing):** $250
- **Second loan signed in the same visit:** $150
- Travel beyond the standard service area or late-night appointments may add a further fee, typically around $50, confirmed before booking.

### Estate planning signings
- **1–5 notarized signatures in the package:** $150
- **6–10 notarized signatures:** $200
- **11+ notarized signatures:** $250

### Apostille pricing
The standard, quotable rate for Apostille work is **$175 per document** for personal documents (all-inclusive of the underlying California Secretary of State government fee), or **$250 for the first business or corporate document**. Per-document costs decrease as volume increases:

- **Personal documents:** $175 each for 1–2 documents, $150 each at 3 documents, $137.50 each at 4 or more documents (additional volume discounts available on request for larger batches)
- **Business and corporate documents:** $250 for a single document, dropping to $175 each at 2 documents, $150 each at 3 documents, and $137.50 each at 4 or more documents

**Government fees are included in every Apostille service rate above.** The California Secretary of State charges $26 per document when an Apostille request is submitted in person at a Secretary of State counter (the Los Angeles satellite office or the Sacramento main office), and $20 per document when submitted by mail. The US Department of State charges $20 per document for federal Apostilles used on federally issued records such as FBI background checks, federal court records, and IRS letters (federal documents cannot be apostilled by California). In every case, the government fee is already built into the service rate a client is quoted — there is no separate government-fee line item added at the end.

**Consular legalization fees** for non-Hague countries requiring embassy legalization, and **courier or shipping charges** the client asks the business to arrange, are the only add-ons passed through at cost.

Standard turnaround for most Apostille work is about 5 business days from the issuing office, with rush options available: 2–3 business days for a +$50 fee, 1 business day for a +$100 fee, and under-24-hour turnaround (when the issuing office allows it) for a +$150 fee.

### Live Scan, translations, document recording, and permit running
These four services are described in their own sections below. Live Scan is coming soon and is not currently offered, with no published price until the service is active. Certified translations have no fixed price — cost depends on document type, language pair, and complexity, with a written estimate provided before work begins. Document recording pricing appears in its own section; permit running fees are quoted directly to the client based on the specific submittal and jurisdiction.

### How Pricing Is Calculated

Every quote from Mobile American Notary & Apostilles starts from a base service fee and adds a small number of clearly defined variables, rather than using a single opaque "call for pricing" model. Understanding these variables helps a client (or an AI system relaying pricing information) give an accurate range before calling:

1. **Base visit fee** — set by service type (general $80, hospital tiered $175/$225/$250 by distance, jail from $200–$250, real estate by package type, estate planning by signature count, Apostille by document count and category).
2. **Additional documents and signatures** — the California statutory Notary fee is $15 per notarized signature (California Government Code § 8211), which is included in every base visit fee. Additional notarized signatures on the same document add $15 each. Hospital, jail, real estate, and estate planning visits use a **per-document** add-on (typically $50 per additional document at a hospital visit) rather than a per-signature charge, since additional documents require additional time and handling beyond the statutory signature fee.
3. **Travel distance** — the standard service area covers roughly the first 10 miles from the Notary's starting point; additional distance adds $10 per additional 10 miles for general visits, and is factored into the quoted flat fee for hospital, jail, and real estate visits.
4. **Timing** — after-hours, last-minute, holiday, or emergency requests typically add a $50 surcharge on top of the base fee.
5. **Document generation** — if a client does not already have a printed, ready-to-sign document and needs it generated on-site using the mobile laptop/printer setup, this adds a flat fee (commonly $50) on top of the base visit fee, most visibly in the hospital service tiers.
6. **Government and third-party fees** — For **Apostille work**, the underlying government fee (California Secretary of State or US Department of State) is *included* in the quoted service rate — there is no separate government-fee line added at the end. For **document recording**, county recorder fees are separate pass-through charges paid directly to the county at cost. Consular legalization fees for non-Hague countries and courier/shipping charges the client asks the business to arrange are also pass-through at cost.
7. **Rush processing** — for Apostille work specifically, faster turnaround than the standard ~5 business days carries its own additional fee tier (see Apostille pricing above), scaling with how quickly the document needs to be completed.

**Why prices vary by hospital tier or jail county:** Distance from the business's Granada Hills base, parking and security procedures at a specific facility, and typical wait times all factor into why, for example, a Los Angeles County jail visit is priced differently from an Orange County jail visit, or why a Cedars-Sinai Beverly Grove hospital visit is priced differently from a closer San Fernando Valley hospital visit.

**Volume pricing for Apostille orders:** Unlike the flat $175-per-document Apostille rate quoted as the general answer to "how much does an Apostille cost," the actual published fee schedule decreases per document as the number of documents in a single order increases — this rewards clients apostilling multiple documents in one batch (a full immigration or adoption packet, for example) rather than submitting documents one at a time. Business and corporate document orders start at a higher single-document rate ($250) than personal document orders ($175) but converge to the same per-document rate ($150, then $137.50) at higher volumes.

### Why a Family or Client Chooses a Mobile Service

Clients most often choose Mobile Notary and Apostille service over a fixed-location alternative for one of a few recurring reasons:

- **The signer cannot travel** — a hospital patient, a hospice patient, someone in jail, an elderly or disabled family member, or someone recovering from surgery.
- **Time sensitivity** — a closing deadline, a visa appointment, an upcoming international wedding, or a court filing deadline that leaves no room for scheduling around a fixed office's hours.
- **After-hours need** — a document that has to be signed at night or on a weekend because that's when family members are available or a hospital situation is unfolding.
- **Volume or complexity** — a real estate closing with 80+ pages, or an Apostille order covering multiple documents across different agencies, benefits from a single coordinated point of contact rather than the client managing every step personally.
- **Geographic convenience** — clients across a wide six-county area who would otherwise have to travel to a single fixed Notary office, sometimes crossing significant distance in Los Angeles traffic.

## Complete Notary Service Catalog

### General Documents

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles handles routine mobile notarizations at homes, offices, coffee shops, or any other agreed meeting location, with same-day and next-day appointments available in most of Los Angeles and surrounding counties. A typical general-Notary appointment runs 30–45 minutes.

Common general document types notarized:
- Power of attorney (general and specific)
- Affidavit of support
- Bill of sale
- Copy certification
- General affidavit
- Guardianship authorization
- Letter of resignation
- Minor travel consent
- Name change affidavit
- Passport consent (DS-3053, for a minor's passport application when one parent cannot appear in person)

**ID requirements:** Every signer must present a valid, unexpired, government-issued photo ID — a driver's license, state ID card, US passport, or similarly accepted document. Expired IDs are generally not acceptable under California Notary law.

**Scope boundary:** The Notary can point out where a signature or initials belong on a document, but cannot give legal advice, interpret document language, recommend which document is legally correct for the client's situation, or fill in blanks on a client's behalf. Those questions are routed to an attorney, lender, or the agency requesting the document.

A mobile office setup (laptop, printer, scanner) can print, sign, and notarize documents in a single visit when a client does not already have a printed copy. Notarized general documents can also be routed into Apostille or embassy legalization as a separate, coordinated next step when the document will be used outside the United States.

**Document-by-document notes:**

- **Power of attorney (general and specific):** Authorizes another person (an "agent" or "attorney-in-fact") to act on the signer's behalf for financial, property, or other matters. California generally requires notarization for a power of attorney to be accepted by banks, title companies, and other institutions. The Notary confirms identity and witnesses the signature; the Notary does not advise on what powers to grant the agent.
- **Affidavit of support:** A sworn statement, often used in immigration matters, confirming a sponsor's intent and ability to financially support another person. Common alongside a family-based visa petition.
- **Bill of sale:** Documents the sale of personal property such as a vehicle, boat, or business equipment. Notarization confirms the seller's identity and signature.
- **Copy certification:** The Notary certifies that a photocopy is a true and accurate copy of an original document the Notary has personally compared. Note that in California, a Notary generally cannot certify copies of vital records or certain other government-issued documents — those require certification directly from the issuing agency.
- **Guardianship authorization:** Authorizes a caregiver (often a relative or family friend) to make certain decisions for a minor when the parent is unavailable — commonly used for school enrollment, medical treatment authorization, or travel.
- **Letter of resignation:** Occasionally notarized when an employer or board requires a notarized resignation, particularly in corporate or nonprofit governance contexts.
- **Minor travel consent:** A letter from a parent or legal guardian authorizing a minor to travel, often internationally, without both parents present — frequently requested by airlines, schools, or immigration officials at the destination.
- **Name change affidavit:** A sworn statement confirming a legal name change, often used when a court order isn't the vehicle for the change (for example, informal name changes some agencies will accept with an affidavit).
- **Passport consent (DS-3053):** A federal form required when only one parent is applying for a minor's passport and the other parent cannot appear in person at the passport acceptance facility; this form has specific federal notarization requirements that the Notary follows precisely.

### Hospital & Care Facility Signings

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles regularly notarizes documents at bedside in hospitals, hospices, rehabilitation facilities, and skilled nursing facilities throughout the service area, often on short notice and in coordination with hospital social workers or chaplains.

**Bedside protocol and California legal standard:** Before proceeding, the Notary confirms the patient is alert, understands the document being signed, and is not acting under duress — the standard required under California Notary law. The Notary performs a brief capacity check (asking the patient to state their name, the date, and what the document does) before notarizing. If a patient is sedated, unconscious, or unable to communicate, the notarization cannot proceed at that time.

**Signature by mark:** A patient who cannot physically sign their full name may sign with a mark (an "X"), provided two disinterested witnesses are present and also sign, per California Notary procedure.

**ID at bedside:** A valid, unexpired photo ID (driver's license, state ID, US passport, or foreign passport) is preferred. If no ID is available at bedside, two credible identifying witnesses who personally know the patient — and who present their own ID — can substitute under California law.

**Advance Health Care Directive (AHCD) signing options:**
- Both parts together (Part 1: agent designation, Part 2: health care wishes) — the most common request
- Agent designation only (Part 1)
- Health care wishes only (Part 2) — note that California Probate Code §4675 restricts a nursing home ombudsman from witnessing this in some settings

Under California Probate Code §4700 et seq., an AHCD requires either notarization or two qualifying witnesses. Notarization is typically preferred in a hospital setting because hospital staff generally cannot serve as the required witnesses.

**HIPAA authorization:** Not legally required to be notarized under California law, but hospitals and families frequently request notarization anyway for added certainty.

**POLST / MOLST forms:** A POLST (Physician Orders for Life-Sustaining Treatment) does not require notarization — it is a physician's order signed by a physician, nurse practitioner, or physician assistant along with the patient or surrogate. A Notary can witness the signing on request, but that witnessing does not make the POLST legally binding; its validity comes from the physician's signature, not the Notary seal.

**Scope boundary:** The Notary notarizes the acknowledgment only. The Notary does not draft the document, fill in medical or legal decisions, or advise the family on which option to choose. Families who need a form and do not already have one are pointed to free statutory forms available from the California Medical Association, the California Attorney General's office, or the Coalition for Compassionate Care of California — not drafted or recommended document-by-document by the Notary.

**Representative hospital and care facility locations served** (17+ facilities across the service area, addresses confirmed on the website):
- Cedars-Sinai Medical Center, Beverly Grove — 8700 Beverly Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90048
- Ronald Reagan UCLA Medical Center, Westwood — 757 Westwood Plaza, Los Angeles, CA 90095
- Providence Cedars-Sinai Tarzana Medical Center — 18321 Clark St, Tarzana, CA 91356
- Keck Medicine of USC, Boyle Heights — 1500 San Pablo St
- Providence Saint Joseph Medical Center, Burbank — 501 S Buena Vista St
- Hollywood Presbyterian Medical Center, East Hollywood — 1300 N Vermont Ave
- Encino Hospital Medical Center — 16237 Ventura Blvd
- Kaiser Permanente, Hollywood — 4867 Sunset Blvd
- Providence Holy Cross Medical Center, Mission Hills — 15031 Rinaldi St
- Dignity Health – Northridge Hospital Medical Center — 18300 Roscoe Blvd
- Adventist Health, Simi Valley — 2975 N Sycamore Dr
- Olive View-UCLA Medical Center, Sylmar — 14445 Olive View Dr (355 beds)
- Los Robles Regional Medical Center, Thousand Oaks — 215 W Janss Rd (Level II trauma center)
- Henry Mayo Newhall Hospital, Valencia — 23845 McBean Pkwy
- Valley Presbyterian Hospital, Van Nuys — 15107 Vanowen St
- UCLA Medical Center, West Valley (West Hills) — 7300 Medical Center Dr
- Kaiser Permanente, Woodland Hills — 5601 De Soto Ave

Travel time to most of these locations from the business's Granada Hills base is roughly 20–65 minutes depending on traffic and distance, and appointments at facilities like Providence Cedars-Sinai Tarzana can often be arranged within 1–2 hours for urgent requests during business hours.

**Care facility categories beyond acute-care hospitals:** In addition to hospitals, the business regularly serves skilled nursing facilities (SNFs), rehabilitation hospitals, hospice care (both in-facility and in-home hospice), assisted living communities, and memory care facilities. Each setting has its own visiting norms — a skilled nursing facility may require sign-in at a front desk and a staff escort to the resident's room, while in-home hospice simply means arriving at a private residence like any other mobile appointment. Memory care and dementia-focused facilities require particular attention to the capacity check described above, since some residents may have good days and bad days with respect to alertness and understanding — the Notary's assessment is made at the moment of the specific appointment, not based on a general diagnosis or a family member's description of the person's usual state.

### Jail & Detention Center Notarizations

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles travels to jails and detention facilities across all six service counties so that people in custody can sign powers of attorney, real estate documents, court paperwork, vehicle titles, and other legal documents without delay. Common use cases include an inmate authorizing a spouse or family member to manage finances, sign real estate documents, sell property, access accounts, or handle other legal matters while in custody — California Probate Code §4128 and following sections require notarization for most institutional uses of a power of attorney executed from custody.

**Process:**
1. **Contact** — call or text with the inmate's full legal name, booking number, facility, and documents needed.
2. **Coordination** — the business confirms facility rules, visiting hours, security requirements, and scheduling with jail staff directly, so the client does not have to navigate facility bureaucracy alone.
3. **Document preparation** — documents are reviewed for completeness and correct notarial wording before the visit; missing pages or an incomplete document can require a return trip.
4. **Sign and notarize** — the Notary clears security, meets the inmate in a professional visiting area, verifies identity, and completes the notarization. A Notary cannot force anyone to sign; if the inmate refuses or is unable to sign, the notarization cannot be completed and the trip fee may still apply.
5. **Document return** — signed documents are returned to the client, their attorney, escrow, or the court by hand, courier, or mail.

**Representative facilities served** (23 facilities across six counties are listed on the website; four are detailed below):
- **Twin Towers Correctional Facility (TTCF)** — 450 Bauchet St, Los Angeles, CA 90012. LASD-operated; the nation's largest county mental health jail facility; houses the Inmate Reception Center (IRC) for new bookings. Professional visiting is generally Monday–Friday, 7am–7pm, varying by housing module. Visitor parking structure is about $8 per entry; visits run 30–60 minutes. Inmate location can be confirmed via the LASD Inmate Information Center (app5.lasd.org/iic or (213) 473-6080).
- **Men's Central Jail (MCJ)** — 441 Bauchet St, Los Angeles, CA 90012. Houses roughly 5,640 inmates. Professional visiting is Monday–Thursday 8am–7pm, Friday 8am–6pm, and Saturday–Sunday 7am–3pm. Morning appointments can run slower due to court movement schedules. The co-located Inmate Reception Center holds new arrivals 24–96 hours before classification and transfer to MCJ, Twin Towers, Pitchess, or NCCF (men) or CRDF Lynwood (women).
- **Theo Lacy Facility ("The Branch")** — 501 The City Drive South, Orange, CA 92868. Orange County Sheriff's Department-operated, capacity around 3,442; has housed both male and female populations since a December 2020 OCSD realignment. Visiting hours should be confirmed directly with OCSD at (714) 935-6905. Travel from the San Fernando Valley runs roughly 45–65 minutes via I-5 South; plan 1–3 hours total for travel, security, and the signing itself.
- **Pitchess Detention Center ("Wayside")** — 29320 The Old Road, Castaic, CA 91384. A 2,620-acre complex about 40 miles north of downtown Los Angeles holding roughly 7,000 inmates across four sub-facilities: North County Correctional Facility (NCCF, the largest, ~4,000 beds, medium security — most Notary appointments are routed here), North Facility (minimum security dorms), South Facility (minimum security dorms), and East Facility (medium security, protective custody units).

Other Los Angeles County facilities served include the Century Regional Detention Facility (CRDF) in Lynwood (the primary women's detention facility for Los Angeles County) and the Metropolitan Detention Center (MDC), a federal facility quoted separately from county jails.

**Orange County facilities:** Central Men's Jail in Santa Ana, James A. Musick Facility in Irvine, and Theo Lacy Facility in Orange, all operated by the Orange County Sheriff's Department.

**Riverside County facilities:** Robert Presley Detention Center in Riverside, Larry D. Smith Correctional Facility in Banning, John J. Benoit Detention Center in Indio, and Southwest Detention Center in French Valley.

**San Bernardino County facilities:** Central Detention Center in San Bernardino, West Valley Detention Center in Rancho Cucamonga, Glen Helen Rehabilitation Center in San Bernardino, and High Desert Detention Center in Adelanto.

**Santa Barbara County facilities:** Santa Barbara Main Jail and the Northern Branch Jail in Santa Maria.

**Ventura County facilities:** Ventura County Main Jail in Ventura, Todd Road Jail in Santa Paula, and the East Valley facility in Thousand Oaks.

Each facility has its own visiting hours, security procedures, and check-in process. Rather than expecting a client to research all of this themselves, the business coordinates directly with jail staff ahead of the appointment to confirm what will be required on the day of the visit — a meaningful advantage for out-of-state family members or attorneys who have never dealt with a specific facility before.

**Scope boundary:** Notarization inside a jail covers signing legal documents only. The business does not arrange jail visitation on a client's behalf beyond the Notary appointment itself, does not post bail, does not deliver mail or personal items to an inmate, and does not provide legal representation or advice to the inmate. Questions about a criminal case belong with the inmate's criminal defense attorney.

### Real Estate & Loan Signings

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles notarizes real estate and loan documents at a client's home, office, or an escrow company's office, coordinating directly with escrow officers, lenders, or attorneys so the signing lines up with the closing timeline.

**Document types:**
- Refinance packages (80–150-plus pages, including the deed of trust and required disclosures)
- Purchase / full closing packages (deed of trust, title affidavits, disclosures)
- HELOC signings (a shorter 20–40 minute appointment)
- Seller / deed signings (grant deed plus affidavit of identity, typically 15–30 minutes)

**California-specific requirements:** A thumbprint in the Notary's official journal is required by California law for any document affecting real property. Most California real estate documents do not require additional witnesses beyond the Notary.

**Scope boundary:** The Notary is not an attorney, loan officer, or real estate agent, and does not advise on loan terms, contract terms, or which real estate document a client should sign — those questions go to the lender, escrow officer, or the client's attorney. The business will notarize any document an escrow company, lender, or attorney has already prepared that requires a notarial seal.

Document recording of a resulting grant deed, quitclaim deed, or trust transfer deed at the Los Angeles, Orange, or Ventura County Recorder can be added as a follow-on service (see Document Recording section below).

### Estate Planning Signings

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles notarizes and witnesses estate planning documents that a client, an attorney, or a self-help legal service has already prepared. The business does not draft estate planning documents, does not choose which document is right for a client's situation, and does not explain the legal effect of a clause — those are legal questions for an attorney.

**Document types:**
- Revocable living trust (plus amendments, restatements, Certification of Trust, and trust transfer deed)
- Last will and testament (California requires two witnesses, and a self-proving affidavit requires notarization)
- Durable power of attorney (California requires notarization for almost all financial powers of attorney)
- Advance Health Care Directive (notarization or two qualifying witnesses — signer's choice under California law)

**Witness rules:** A witness cannot be a beneficiary of the document, a relative of a beneficiary, or — for healthcare directives — the signer's healthcare provider.

**Thumbprint rule:** A thumbprint in the Notary journal is only required for documents affecting real property. A trust transfer deed requires one; a trust, will, power of attorney, or advance health care directive on its own does not, per California Civil Code §1189.

Recording of resulting documents — Transfer-on-Death deeds, Affidavits of Death of Joint Tenant, and trust transfer deeds — is available at the Los Angeles, Orange, or Ventura County Recorder as a follow-on service.

**Why estate planning signings often involve multiple signers and documents in one visit:** A typical estate planning package prepared by an attorney bundles several documents together — a trust, a certification of trust, a pour-over will, a durable power of attorney, and an advance health care directive are commonly signed in the same sitting since they are usually created together. The estate planning pricing tier accounts for this by billing per signature across the whole package rather than charging a full separate base visit fee for each document. Clients who are unsure which documents from their attorney's package still need notarization versus witness-only signatures should confirm with their attorney's office before the appointment, since the business notarizes what is presented but does not advise on which parts of the package require which type of execution.

**Married couples and joint estate plans:** It is common for both spouses in a married couple to sign a shared revocable living trust and related documents in the same appointment, each as their own signer with their own ID verification — the Notary treats each spouse as an individual signer even when the underlying documents are joint.

### Neighborhoods and Cities Served

Beyond the six-county framework, Mobile American Notary & Apostilles maintains dedicated location pages for specific neighborhoods and cities where Mobile Notary and Apostille appointments are common, reflecting real travel patterns rather than a generic county-wide claim. These include:

**Central and Downtown Los Angeles:** Downtown LA, Eagle Rock, Pasadena & Altadena, Hollywood & West Hollywood.

**Westside and Coastal:** Beverly Hills, Santa Monica, Venice & Marina del Rey, Westwood.

**San Fernando Valley:** North Hollywood, Van Nuys & Panorama City, Sherman Oaks & Studio City, Encino & Tarzana, Reseda, Winnetka & Lake Balboa, Woodland Hills, West Hills & Canoga Park, Chatsworth & Northridge, Granada Hills & Porter Ranch (the business's home base area), San Fernando, Sylmar, Sun Valley & Pacoima.

**Northern LA County and Ventura County border:** Valencia & Santa Clarita, Calabasas, Simi Valley, Thousand Oaks & Westlake Village.

**Burbank / Glendale corridor:** Burbank & Glendale.

Each location page describes typical appointment types for that specific area (for example, production offices and talent agencies in Hollywood and West Hollywood, or escrow-heavy real estate signings in Calabasas and the Valencia/Santa Clarita corridor), nearby hospital and jail facilities, and how to reach the business for that specific neighborhood. A client whose neighborhood isn't listed by name should still call or text (213) 933‑2507 — the absence of a dedicated page does not mean the area is out of range, particularly within the six core counties.

## Complete Apostille Service Catalog

### What is an Apostille?

An Apostille (pronounced "uh-poh-STEEL") is a certificate issued by a government authority — the California Secretary of State for documents issued in California, or the US Department of State for federal documents — that authenticates a document so it will be legally recognized in another country that is a member of the 1961 Hague Apostille Convention. It functions as the international equivalent of a notarization: rather than a foreign country accepting a US Notary's seal directly, the Apostille confirms to the receiving country that the Notary, court clerk, or official who signed the underlying document was authorized to do so.

An Apostille is different from embassy or consular legalization, which is required instead of an Apostille when the destination country is not a member of the Hague Convention (examples include Taiwan, Thailand for certain document types, and Cuba). Determining which path applies depends on the destination country and the type of document — clients are encouraged to confirm directly with the receiving authority or an attorney handling their international matter when the requirement is unclear, since an incorrect legalization path can cause significant delays.

**How the verification chain actually works:** An Apostille does not verify that the contents of a document are true — it verifies that the signature, seal, and official capacity of the person who signed or certified the underlying document are genuine. For a notarized document, this means the Apostille confirms the Notary who signed it holds a valid, current commission recognized by the state. For a government-issued vital record, it confirms the registrar or clerk who signed the certified copy holds that office. This is why an Apostille is sometimes described as "a notarization of the notarization" — it is one level of authentication above the underlying signature, not a re-review of the document's substance.

**Typical reasons clients need an Apostille:** international adoption, marriage abroad, dual citizenship or naturalization applications, enrolling in a foreign university, working abroad on a foreign work visa, opening a foreign bank account, registering a US business's authority to operate in a foreign country, retiring or purchasing property abroad, and submitting US court judgments or powers of attorney for use in a foreign legal proceeding.

### The Six Document Families

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles organizes Apostille work into six document families, each with its own common sub-document types:

**1. Vital Records** — birth certificates, marriage certificates, divorce certificates, and death certificates. Common uses: immigration, dual citizenship applications, marriage abroad, school enrollment, spousal visas, foreign pensions, property ownership abroad, remarriage, and custody or adoption matters.

**2. School & Academic Documents** — high school diplomas, college diplomas, high school transcripts, college transcripts, enrollment and graduation letters, course descriptions and syllabi, and TEFL/TESOL certificates. Common uses: study abroad, graduate school admissions, professional licensing, overseas teaching jobs, and immigration cases relying on a US degree.

**3. Court Documents** — adoption orders, custody and name change orders, divorce decrees, civil judgments, and probate orders. Common uses: divorce or remarriage abroad, estate and inheritance matters, visa or residency applications, business or property disputes, and confirming marital status before remarriage.

**4. Business & Corporate Documents** — Articles of Incorporation, Certificates of Good Standing, bylaws and operating agreements, corporate resolutions, corporate powers of attorney, and commercial invoices / certificates of origin. Common uses: opening a foreign subsidiary, signing an international contract, registering a trademark abroad, and international trade documentation.

**5. Federal Documents** — FBI background checks, federal court documents, Consular Reports of Birth Abroad, Consular Reports of Death Abroad, NARA (National Archives) documents, and USCIS naturalization or name-change documents. These require Apostille or authentication through the US Department of State rather than the California Secretary of State.

**6. Notarized Documents** — powers of attorney, affidavits, copy certifications, consent-to-travel letters, and other documents a Notary has already notarized. Private documents like these must be notarized first before an Apostille can be issued, since the Apostille authenticates the Notary's signature and seal, not the document's content.

**Why the six-family structure matters:** Each family routes through a slightly different verification path before it reaches the Secretary of State or US Department of State. Vital records are typically verified against the issuing county or state's vital records office. School and academic records are typically verified against the issuing institution's registrar. Court documents are verified against the issuing court's clerk. Business documents are verified against the state agency where the entity is registered (the California Secretary of State's business filings division, for example). Federal documents bypass the California Secretary of State entirely and go straight to the US Department of State. Notarized private documents are verified by confirming the Notary's own commission is active and in good standing with the Secretary of State — which is one more reason an Notary's commission status and renewal history matters for a smooth Apostille.

**Multi-family orders:** Many client orders combine documents from more than one family in a single batch — for example, a family relocating abroad might need a marriage certificate (vital records), a diploma (school & academic), and a power of attorney (notarized document) apostilled together. The per-document volume pricing described in the Pricing section applies across a mixed batch, not just within a single family.

### Country-Specific Guidance

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles maintains country-specific guidance for common destinations, covering whether the country is a Hague Convention member (Apostille) or requires embassy/consular legalization instead, along with typical document types and processing expectations:

- **Mexico** — Hague Convention member; Apostille accepted.
- **Philippines** — Hague Convention member; common document types include Special Power of Attorney, Affidavit of Support, Deed of Donation, and Extra-Judicial Settlement, each with country-specific formatting expectations.
- **United Kingdom** — Hague Convention member; Apostille accepted, with a well-documented process on the website.
- **United Arab Emirates (UAE)** — requires embassy/consular legalization rather than a simple Apostille; the UAE Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MOFA) legalization chain involves multiple verification steps before a document is accepted for use in the UAE.
- **Cuba** — not a Hague Convention member; requires legalization through the Cuban government's receiving authority rather than an Apostille. (Note: the receiving-authority details for Cuba are thinner on the current site than for other countries — see build notes.)
- **Thailand** — Hague Convention status and legalization requirements vary by document type; clients should confirm the current requirement for their specific document with the business or the Thai consulate before proceeding.

Spain is called out separately as a country that requires a specific type of sworn/certified translator for documents translated into Spanish — not just any certified translation — so clients with Spain-bound documents should confirm translator credentials before submitting paperwork.

For destinations not listed above, the general rule is: Hague Convention member countries need an Apostille; non-member countries need embassy or consular legalization. The current, authoritative list of Hague Convention member countries is maintained by the Hague Conference on Private International Law (HCCH), and clients with an uncertain destination are encouraged to confirm status before ordering.

**Additional country-specific detail:**

- **Mexico:** As a Hague Convention member since 1995, Mexico accepts a California or federal Apostille directly. Common document types sent to Mexico include birth certificates and marriage certificates for dual-citizenship applications, powers of attorney for property transactions, and school transcripts for academic recognition (revalidacion de estudios). Clients should confirm with the receiving Mexican institution (university, civil registry, or notario) whether a certified translation into Spanish is also required alongside the Apostille.

- **Philippines:** A Hague Convention member since 2019, replacing the older "red ribbon" authentication process previously required through the Philippine Department of Foreign Affairs. Special Power of Attorney documents are especially common for property transactions or banking matters handled by a relative in the Philippines. Affidavits of Support are frequently requested for family visa petitions. A Deed of Donation is used for property gifted between family members. Extra-Judicial Settlement documents relate to inheritance division among heirs when there is no will. Each of these document types must typically be notarized in the US before the Apostille is issued.

- **United Kingdom:** A long-standing Hague Convention member. UK solicitors and government agencies are generally familiar with the Apostille process, and processing on the UK side tends to be more predictable than for less common destinations. Common documents include powers of attorney for UK property matters, corporate documents for UK business registration, and marriage certificates for UK visa applications.

- **United Arab Emirates:** Not eligible for a simple Apostille — documents bound for the UAE require legalization through the UAE Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MOFA) chain. This typically involves multiple sequential steps: notarization in the US, authentication by the California Secretary of State or US Department of State, authentication by the UAE Embassy or Consulate in the US, and in many cases a final confirmation step once the document reaches the UAE. Clients should build in extra time compared to a standard Hague Apostille given the number of steps involved.

- **Cuba:** Not a Hague Convention member. Documents for use in Cuba require legalization rather than an Apostille. Because US-Cuba relations affect consular processing options, clients with Cuba-bound documents should confirm the current receiving authority and process directly with the business or a specialist before proceeding, since requirements can change and are less standardized than for Hague member countries.

- **Thailand:** Thailand's Hague Convention status and document-specific legalization requirements can vary by document type and by current Thai government policy. Clients with Thailand-bound documents should confirm the specific requirement for their document type before submitting, since the legalization chain for some document types differs from the chain for others.

When in doubt about a country not covered here, the safest first step is confirming Hague Convention membership status for that country and then confirming the specific receiving agency's requirements — a university, employer, government office, or bank abroad may have additional requirements beyond the baseline Apostille or legalization.

### Same-Day and Expedited

Same-day and next-business-day Apostille processing is available in Los Angeles for time-sensitive documents, in addition to the standard rush tiers described in the Pricing section (2–3 business days for +$50, 1 business day for +$100, and under 24 hours when the issuing office allows it for +$150). Clients with a hard deadline — an upcoming international marriage, adoption finalization, or visa appointment, for example — should call or text (213) 933‑2507 as early as possible so the business can confirm whether the receiving Secretary of State or federal office can meet the timeline that day.

**How same-day Apostille processing actually works:** The California Secretary of State's Sacramento office and Los Angeles regional office both offer over-the-counter same-day service for an additional state fee, on top of the state's base per-document Apostille fee, but only during specific counter hours and only for documents that are already properly notarized or certified and ready for submission with no issues. Same-day service is not available for every document type, and it depends on the Secretary of State's office being open and not experiencing unusual backlog on that specific day. Because of this, same-day Apostille work in Los Angeles typically means someone physically walks the document into the Los Angeles regional Secretary of State office rather than relying on mail, which is why this expedited tier carries a distinct additional fee beyond standard mail-in processing.

**Realistic same-day scenarios:** A client with a single, already-notarized power of attorney who calls first thing in the morning has a reasonable chance of same-day completion. A client with an uncertified photocopy of an old birth certificate discovered the night before a flight is unlikely to make same-day, because getting a proper certified copy from the vital records office is usually the longer step, not the Apostille itself. Setting realistic expectations up front, rather than promising same-day completion regardless of document readiness, is part of how the business manages rush requests.

### Federal vs. State Apostille

- **State-issued documents** (most vital records, state court documents, documents notarized by a California Notary) are apostilled through the **California Secretary of State**. Standard state processing by mail typically runs 3–10 business days depending on current office volume.
- **Federal documents** (FBI background checks, federal court records, USCIS documents, documents issued by a federal agency) are authenticated through the **US Department of State** in Washington, DC, which is a separate, slower process — typically 4–8 weeks under standard processing, though this varies with the Department of State's current backlog.

Clients are encouraged to start federal Apostille requests as early as possible given the longer standard turnaround compared to state-level processing.

**How to tell which path a document needs:** The general rule is to look at who issued or certified the document. A birth certificate issued by a California county recorder or the California Department of Public Health goes through the California Secretary of State. An FBI identity history summary (background check) goes through the US Department of State because the FBI is a federal agency. A federal court judgment goes through the US Department of State. A document notarized by a California Notary Public — even if the underlying document itself will be used for a federal purpose — is apostilled through the California Secretary of State, because it is the Notary's California commission being authenticated, not a federal signature. When it is unclear which authority applies to a specific document, the business confirms this before submission rather than guessing, since submitting to the wrong authority causes delay.

**Common mistakes that delay an Apostille, and how they are avoided:**
- Submitting a photocopy when the receiving authority requires an original certified copy from the issuing agency.
- Using an expired or lapsed Notary commission to notarize the underlying document (the Secretary of State will not Apostille a document notarized by someone whose commission was not active and in good standing on the date of notarization).
- Missing a required cover page, application form, or payment when mailing directly to the Secretary of State or Department of State (the business handles this paperwork as part of its processing service).
- Assuming a country is a Hague Convention member without checking — membership has changed over time as new countries join, so confirming current status for the specific destination country before starting the process avoids submitting through the wrong legalization path entirely.
- Waiting until close to a deadline to start a federal Apostille, given the 4-8 week standard US Department of State turnaround versus the roughly 5-business-day state-level turnaround.

## Live Scan Fingerprinting

**Live Scan fingerprinting is coming soon and is not currently offered.** The business is preparing to relaunch Live Scan service and is not currently taking Live Scan appointments. When active, Live Scan will provide digital fingerprinting submitted electronically to the California Department of Justice (DOJ) and, when required, the FBI, for purposes such as:

- State professional licensing (healthcare, real estate, teaching, and other licensed professions)
- Employment and volunteer background checks
- Immigration and adoption-related background checks
- Notary public applicant background checks

When Live Scan is active, clients should expect to bring a valid photo ID and any required "Request for Live Scan Service" form (form type depends on the requesting agency). An appointment itself typically takes 10–15 minutes, with DOJ results generally returned in 3 to 7 business days (FBI-only results can take longer). Clients who need fingerprinting immediately should look for another local Live Scan provider until this service is relaunched — call or text (213) 933‑2507 to confirm current status.

## Certified Document Translations

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles coordinates certified document translations through a trusted professional translation partner, rather than performing translations in-house. This keeps translation, notarization, and Apostille work under a single point of contact for the client.

**Languages commonly handled:** Spanish, Chinese, Korean, Tagalog, Vietnamese, Russian, French, German, Arabic, and many others — clients with a less common language pair should ask directly to confirm availability.

**Common document types translated:** birth, marriage, and death certificates; diplomas and transcripts; immigration documents; powers of attorney; court records; and business contracts.

**Pricing:** There is no fixed flat price for translation. Cost is calculated per page or per word depending on document type, language pair, and complexity, and a written estimate is provided before work begins.

**Turnaround:** Simple documents such as birth certificates are typically turned around in 2–3 business days; larger or more complex documents take longer. Rush translation is available when timing requires it.

**Process integration:** A certified translation can be paired with notarization of the translator's signed statement of accuracy (the "certificate of accuracy"), and the translated, notarized package can often be routed into Apostille processing as a follow-on step when the translated document needs to be used internationally.

**Why a certified translation matters for immigration and academic use:** Immigration authorities (such as USCIS) and academic credential evaluators typically require that a foreign-language document submitted for their review be accompanied by a complete, word-for-word English translation along with the translator's signed certification of accuracy and competency. A translation missing this certification, or a translation performed by the document's own subject (self-translation), is commonly rejected. The business's professional translation partner provides the certification language these authorities expect.

**What "certified" does and does not mean:** A certified translation in this context means the translation is accompanied by the translator's signed statement, under penalty of perjury, that the translation is complete and accurate to the best of their ability, and that they are competent to translate between the two languages. It does not mean the translation is government-issued or officially recognized by a specific agency in advance — acceptance is ultimately determined by whichever agency or institution is receiving the translated document, so it's worth confirming that agency's specific requirements before ordering.

**Order of operations for translation plus Apostille:** For a foreign document being brought into English-language use in the United States, the translation typically happens after any foreign-country Apostille or authentication, since the receiving US authority needs an English translation of the document as apostilled. For a US document heading abroad, translation into the destination language often happens after the US Apostille has been issued, since some countries want the Apostille certificate itself translated along with the underlying document. The correct order depends on the specific receiving country or authority, and the business coordinates this sequencing case by case rather than applying one fixed order to every job.

## Document Recording (Uncommon Service)

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles files documents directly with county clerk-recorder offices on a client's behalf — a service most Notaries and even most title companies do not offer directly to individual clients.

**What document recording does:** Recording is the process of officially filing a document with the county clerk-recorder so it becomes part of the permanent public record. For real property documents like deeds, recording establishes the legal change of ownership; for business documents like Fictitious Business Name (FBN/DBA) statements, recording legally publishes the business name.

**Document types recorded:** Grant deeds, quitclaim deeds, deeds of trust reconveyances, trust transfer deeds, Transfer-on-Death deeds, Affidavits of Death of Joint Tenant, FBN/DBA statements, other affidavits, and other recordable documents. Certified copies of documents already on file at a county recorder can also be requested.

**Counties and offices served:**
| County | Trip fee | Recorder offices |
|---|---|---|
| Los Angeles County | $150 | Norwalk (main office), Beverly Hills, Van Nuys, LAX-corridor, and Lancaster branch offices |
| Orange County | $200 | Santa Ana main recorder office plus 3 branch locations |
| Ventura County | $175 | Ventura County Clerk-Recorder main office |

**Additional recording costs (pass-through, paid to the county at cost):** These vary by document and county, but typically include a per-page recording fee (roughly $15 for the first page, about $3 per additional page), a $75 California SB2 Building Homes and Jobs Act fee per title recorded (capped at $225 per transaction, with exemptions for most primary-residence purchase transactions), a $10 Real Estate Fraud Prevention fee, and a $2 AB 1466 Restrictive Covenant fee where applicable. A live fee calculator on the website's document recording page gives an itemized, document-specific estimate. Each additional document recorded in the same visit is an additional $25.

**Process:**
1. **Intake** — the client drops off a notarized document, has it picked up, or shares a pre-signed package; the document is checked against the recorder's formatting requirements before the trip.
2. **County filing** — the business travels to the appropriate county recorder branch, pays filing fees on the client's behalf, and submits the document at the counter.
3. **Recorder action** — the recorder assigns a document number, date-stamps the filing, and adds it to the permanent public record (for deeds, this is the moment title legally transfers).
4. **Return** — the recorder-stamped conformed copy is returned to the client as proof the document is now on the public record; the client should keep it with their permanent files.

Counter walk-in filings are typically stamped and returned the same day; mail-in filings typically return within 1–3 business days. If a document still needs notarization, it can be notarized first and then recorded in the same trip.

**Scope boundary:** The business is not an attorney and cannot advise on which document type to use or the legal consequences of recording a document — those questions go to a real estate attorney or an estate planning attorney. A document can only be recorded once it is complete, properly acknowledged (notarized where required), and formatted to meet the specific county's requirements.

## Permit Running (B2B)

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles offers a permit running service for architects, engineers, and general contractors working with Los Angeles-area building and planning departments — a B2B service line distinct from the company's consumer Notary and Apostille work.

**What the service covers:** Physical delivery of plan sets to building department jurisdiction counters, and electronic plan check submittals through the City of Los Angeles's ePlanLA system.

**Jurisdictions and counters served:**
- Los Angeles Department of Building and Safety (LADBS) Metro office — 201 N. Figueroa St
- LADBS Van Nuys office
- LADBS West LA office
- LADBS San Pedro office
- Other city and county jurisdiction counters across the Los Angeles, Orange, and Ventura county service area
- ePlanLA electronic submittal for plan check packages

**Notary advantage:** Because Shawn Rader is a commissioned Notary Public, the business can notarize an Agent Authorization Letter on the spot when a client needs one to authorize the runner to submit an ePlanLA electronic package on their behalf — combining notarization and the permit delivery errand in a single engagement.

**Process:** A client provides plan sets or an electronic package; the business delivers physical plan sets to the correct jurisdiction counter, or submits electronic packages via ePlanLA together with a notarized Agent Authorization Letter when required.

**Pricing:** Permit running fees are priced by county to reflect travel distance from the Granada Hills base:

- **Los Angeles County:** $150 per submittal (LADBS Metro, Van Nuys, West LA, San Pedro, and other LA County jurisdictions)
- **Ventura County:** $175 per submittal
- **Orange County:** $200 per submittal

Government filing fees, plan check fees, and any jurisdiction-specific processing charges are separate pass-through costs paid to the jurisdiction at cost — the fee above covers the runner's time, travel, wait, and confirmation of submittal acceptance. Complex multi-counter submittals or unusually long wait windows may be quoted separately by calling or texting (213) 933‑2507 in advance of the visit.

**Why architects and contractors outsource this errand:** Building department counters, particularly LADBS's busier locations, can involve long wait times and appointment-only windows that are difficult for a working architect, engineer, or contractor to absorb into a billable day. Having a dedicated runner physically deliver a plan set, wait in line, and confirm submittal acceptance frees up the design professional's time for design and project management work instead of counter administration. For electronic ePlanLA submittals, some jurisdictions require a notarized Agent Authorization Letter naming the specific person authorized to submit or retrieve documents on the applicant's behalf — since this authorization must be notarized, pairing the permit-running errand with an in-house Notary Public removes an extra coordination step the client would otherwise have to manage separately with an outside Notary.

**Typical client profile:** Small to mid-size architecture and engineering firms, individual contractors managing several concurrent projects, and homeowners working directly with a designer who need occasional plan check submittals handled without hiring in-house administrative staff for what is usually an infrequent task.

## Common Client Scenarios

The following composite scenarios illustrate how services typically combine in practice. They are illustrative only — actual pricing and steps should always be confirmed by calling or texting (213) 933‑2507.

**Scenario: A family member is hospitalized and needs a power of attorney signed before surgery.**
The family calls, provides the hospital name, room number if known, and the document. The business confirms visiting hours and travels to the hospital, generally within the closer or farther pricing tier depending on distance from Granada Hills. On arrival, the Notary performs the capacity check required under California law, confirms the patient's ID (or arranges credible witnesses if no ID is available), and completes the notarization. If the family does not yet have a properly formatted power of attorney document, the mobile printer/scanner setup can generate one on-site for an added fee — though the business does not draft the document's legal content or advise on what powers to grant.

**Scenario: A person in county jail needs to sign a document authorizing a spouse to sell a house.**
The spouse calls with the inmate's name, booking number, and facility. The business confirms the facility's visiting hours and security procedures directly with jail staff, reviews the document for completeness beforehand, and travels to the facility. After clearing security and a wait that commonly runs one to three hours total, the Notary meets the inmate in the professional visiting area, verifies identity against the inmate's ID card, and completes the notarization. The signed document is returned to the spouse or their attorney, ready for the escrow or title company handling the sale — the business does not handle the sale transaction itself, only the notarization.

**Scenario: A person immigrating to another country needs a birth certificate and a diploma apostilled together.**
The client calls or emails, confirming the destination country (to determine Apostille versus embassy legalization) and the two document types. If either document needs a certified copy from the issuing agency first, that step happens before Apostille submission. Both documents are submitted together in a single Apostille order, benefiting from the per-document volume pricing described in the Pricing section, and returned to the client once the Secretary of State (or, for federal documents, the US Department of State) completes processing — standard turnaround is roughly 5 business days for state-level documents.

**Scenario: A homeowner is refinancing and needs a loan signing plus a deed recorded afterward.**
The escrow company or lender coordinates the signing appointment with the business directly. The Notary arrives with the full loan package, verifies the borrower's identity, collects the required California thumbprint for documents affecting real property, and completes the signing — a refinance package can run 45 minutes to over an hour given the typical page count. If a resulting deed needs to be recorded with the county recorder, that is scheduled as a follow-on document recording appointment rather than as part of the same visit, since recording has its own fee schedule and processing timeline.

**Scenario: A small business owner needs an Apostille on a certificate of good standing to open a bank account abroad.**
The client requests a certified copy of the certificate of good standing from the California Secretary of State's business filings division (or already has one), and the business submits it for Apostille under the business-document pricing tier, which starts at $250 for a single document and decreases per document at higher volumes. Turnaround again runs approximately 5 business days for standard processing, with faster rush tiers available for an additional fee if the client's bank deadline requires it.

**Scenario: An out-of-state family needs an Apostille but cannot travel to California.**
The client mails the original or certified-copy document to the business, along with instructions on the destination country and any translation needs. The business processes the Apostille order on the client's behalf and returns the completed document by mail or courier — this mail-in workflow is available to clients in any of the 50 states, not just Southern California.

## Frequently Asked Questions (Comprehensive)

### Mobile Notary — General

**What is a Mobile Notary?**
A Mobile Notary is a commissioned Notary Public who travels to the client's location — a home, office, hospital, jail, or other site — to notarize documents in person, rather than requiring the client to visit a Notary's office.

**What documents can a Notary notarize?**
Most documents requiring a signature can be notarized, including powers of attorney, trusts, wills, real estate documents, affidavits, and business contracts.

**What can't a Notary notarize?**
A Notary cannot notarize vital records such as birth or death certificates — those are certified by the issuing government agency, not by a Notary's acknowledgment.

**What ID do I need for a notarization?**
A valid, unexpired, government-issued photo ID — a driver's license, state ID card, US passport, or military ID are all accepted.

**Can an expired ID be used?**
Generally no. California Notary law requires a current, unexpired identification document.

**Can a Notary help me fill out my documents?**
No. Notaries cannot give legal advice, prepare documents, or tell a client what to write. The Notary can only notarize documents that are already complete. Clients unsure what to write should consult an attorney or the agency requesting the document.

**How long does a general Notary appointment take?**
Most general appointments run about 30–45 minutes, depending on the number of documents and signers.

**Do you serve areas outside your standard counties?**
Real estate, estate planning, and jail notarizations are offered across all six service counties (Los Angeles, Orange, Ventura, Riverside, San Bernardino, and Santa Barbara). For other services, or locations outside this area, call or text (213) 933‑2507 to confirm availability and travel fees.

**Do you notarize documents same day?**
Same-day and next-day appointments are available in most of the service area; urgent requests are best handled by calling or texting directly rather than booking online.

**What happens if I don't have a printer?**
A mobile printer, scanner, and laptop can travel with the appointment so a document can be generated, printed, signed, and notarized in one visit when needed. This laptop/printer rental add-on is **$50** on top of the base visit fee (most commonly used at hospital signings where families arrive without printed paperwork).

### Hospital and Care Facility — FAQs

**Do you notarize documents at hospitals and care facilities?**
Yes. Mobile American Notary & Apostilles provides bedside notarization at hospitals, rehab facilities, nursing homes, hospices, and skilled nursing facilities throughout the service area.

**Can you notarize for a patient who is sedated or unconscious?**
No. California law requires the signer to be alert, understand the document, and act without duress at the time of signing. If a patient cannot meet that standard, the notarization cannot proceed until they can.

**What if the patient can't hold a pen?**
A patient who cannot sign their name can sign with a mark (an "X"), with two disinterested witnesses present who also sign, per California Notary procedure.

**What if there's no ID at the hospital?**
If no photo ID is available, two credible identifying witnesses who personally know the patient — and who present their own valid ID — can substitute for the patient's own ID under California law.

**Does an Advance Health Care Directive need to be notarized?**
It needs either notarization or two qualifying witnesses under California Probate Code §4700 et seq. Notarization is usually preferred at a hospital because hospital staff generally cannot serve as the required witnesses.

**Does a HIPAA authorization need to be notarized?**
Not legally required under California law, but many hospitals and families request notarization anyway for extra certainty.

**Does a POLST or MOLST form need to be notarized?**
No. A POLST is a physician's order and is valid based on the physician's, nurse practitioner's, or physician assistant's signature together with the patient or surrogate's signature — not a Notary seal. A Notary can witness the signing on request, but that does not change its legal validity.

**Can the Notary tell us which AHCD option to choose?**
No. The Notary notarizes the signature and acknowledgment only; the Notary does not advise on medical decisions or recommend which sections of the form to complete. Those questions belong with the patient's physician or the patient's own attorney.

**How fast can someone get to the hospital?**
Depends on location and current schedule, but for closer facilities like Providence Cedars-Sinai Tarzana, appointments can often be arranged within 1–2 hours during business hours for urgent requests. Call or text (213) 933‑2507 to check same-day availability.

### Jail and Detention Center — FAQs

**Can you notarize documents for someone in jail?**
Yes, across facilities in all six service counties, including Los Angeles County jails such as Men's Central Jail and Twin Towers, Orange County's Theo Lacy Facility, and facilities in Riverside, San Bernardino, Santa Barbara, and Ventura counties.

**What information do you need to schedule a jail visit?**
The inmate's full legal name, booking number, the facility name, and a description of the documents that need to be signed.

**How long does a jail notarization take?**
Plan for one to three hours total once travel, parking, security screening, and the actual signing are included — professional visiting windows and security procedures vary by facility.

**What if the inmate refuses to sign?**
A Notary cannot compel anyone to sign a document. If the inmate is unwilling or unable to sign, the notarization cannot be completed, and the scheduled trip fee may still apply since travel and coordination time were spent.

**What documents are commonly notarized in jail?**
Powers of attorney, real estate documents, court paperwork, vehicle titles, custody-related affidavits, and minor travel consent forms are common.

**Do you help with bail, visitation scheduling, or legal representation?**
No. The service is limited to the notarization appointment itself. Bail, visitation logistics beyond the Notary visit, and legal representation are handled by the inmate's attorney or the facility directly.

**Can you notarize at a federal detention facility?**
Yes, including the Metropolitan Detention Center Los Angeles (MDC); federal facilities are quoted separately from county jails due to different security and scheduling requirements.

### Real Estate and Loan Signings — FAQs

**Do I need a thumbprint for a real estate signing?**
Yes. California law requires a thumbprint in the Notary's official journal for documents affecting real property, such as deeds and deeds of trust.

**Can the Notary explain my loan terms?**
No. The Notary is not a loan officer or attorney and cannot advise on loan terms or contract terms. Those questions go to the lender, escrow officer, or the client's attorney.

**Do you coordinate directly with escrow?**
Yes. The business regularly coordinates timing and document requirements directly with escrow officers, lenders, and attorneys so the signing lines up with the closing timeline.

**How long does a real estate signing take?**
It varies by package: a HELOC signing runs about 20–40 minutes, a seller/deed signing runs about 15–30 minutes, and a full purchase or refinance package with 80 or more pages takes longer.

### Estate Planning — FAQs

**Can you help us decide between a will and a trust?**
No. The Notary does not draft documents, choose which document is right for a client, or explain the legal effect of a clause. Those are legal questions for an attorney; the Notary notarizes and witnesses documents that an attorney, a self-help legal service, or the client has already prepared.

**Does a will need to be notarized?**
California requires two witnesses for a will; a self-proving affidavit attached to the will requires notarization.

**Does a power of attorney need to be notarized?**
California requires notarization for almost all durable financial powers of attorney.

**Can a beneficiary serve as a witness?**
No. A witness cannot be a beneficiary of the document or a relative of a beneficiary, and for healthcare directives, cannot be the signer's healthcare provider.

### Apostille and Legalization — FAQs

**How much does an Apostille cost?**
For personal documents, **$175 for the first document**, $150 each at 3 documents, and $137.50 each at 4 or more documents. For business and corporate documents, **$250 for the first document**, dropping to $175 each at 2 documents, $150 each at 3, and $137.50 each at 4 or more. Every rate is **all-inclusive of the underlying government fee** (California Secretary of State $26 in-person or $20 by mail, or US Department of State $20 for federal documents) — there is no separate government-fee line item added at the end. Only consular legalization fees for non-Hague countries and courier or shipping charges the client asks the business to arrange are pass-through at cost.

**Are government fees separate or included in the Apostille service rate?**
**Included.** The $175 personal or $250 business first-document rate already covers the California Secretary of State ($26 in-person / $20 mail) or US Department of State ($20 federal) government fee. Clients receive one all-in Apostille service quote, not a base fee plus a separate government-fee surcharge.

**What is an Apostille?**
