A certificate issued by the California Secretary of State (for California-issued documents) or the US Department of State (for federal documents) that authenticates a document for legal use in another Hague Apostille Convention member country.

**How long does an Apostille take in California?**
Standard state-level processing by mail typically runs 3–10 business days. Same-day or next-business-day service is available in Los Angeles for urgent cases, and additional rush tiers are available for a fee (see Pricing).

**How long does a federal Apostille take?**
Federal document authentication through the US Department of State typically takes 4–8 weeks under standard processing, considerably longer than state-level Apostille processing — clients with federal documents should start as early as possible.

**What documents can be apostilled?**
Vital records (birth, marriage, death certificates), school and academic records, court documents, business and corporate documents, federal documents, and notarized private documents like powers of attorney and affidavits.

**Does a private document need to be notarized before it can be apostilled?**
Yes. Private documents such as powers of attorney, affidavits, and corporate resolutions must be notarized first — the Apostille authenticates the Notary's signature and seal, not the underlying content.

**What if my destination country isn't a Hague Convention member?**
Non-Hague countries such as Taiwan, Cuba, and some document types for Thailand require embassy or consular legalization instead of an Apostille — a different, typically slower process involving the destination country's consulate.

**Do you handle documents from states other than California?**
Yes. The business works with clients and documents from all 50 states for both California state-level and federal Apostille processing, including mail-in service for out-of-state clients.

**What's the most common Apostille mistake?**
Common pitfalls include incorrect or incomplete notarization on the underlying document, missing or unclear Notary seals, outdated vital records that no longer match current requirements, sending a document to the wrong government office, and submitting a plain file-stamped court copy that does not meet the issuing authority's certification requirements. Reviewing document requirements with the business before submission helps avoid rejection and delay.

**Do you provide legal advice about international legal requirements?**
No. The business helps clients understand the Apostille and legalization process procedurally but does not give legal advice about a client's specific international legal matter (immigration status, marriage validity abroad, or contract enforceability, for example) — those questions go to an attorney handling the matter, or the receiving foreign authority directly.

### Live Scan — FAQs

**Is Live Scan fingerprinting currently available?**
No. Live Scan is coming soon and is not currently offered. Call or text (213) 933‑2507 to confirm current status before planning around this service.

**What is Live Scan used for when it's active?**
State professional licensing, employment and volunteer background checks, immigration and adoption background checks, and Notary applicant background checks.

**How long do Live Scan results take when the service is active?**
DOJ results are typically returned in 3 to 7 business days; FBI-only results can take longer.

### Certified Translation — FAQs

**How much does a certified translation cost?**
There is no fixed flat price. Cost depends on document type, language pair, and complexity, calculated per page or per word, with a written estimate provided before work begins.

**How long does a translation take?**
Simple documents such as birth certificates typically take 2–3 business days; larger or more complex documents take longer. Rush service is available.

**Do I need to notarize a translated document?**
Not automatically — but the translator's signed certificate of accuracy is often notarized, and the notarized translation package can be routed into Apostille processing when the document needs to be used internationally. Clients should confirm the specific requirement with the receiving authority, since requirements vary by country and by the agency requesting the document.

**What languages do you support?**
Spanish, Chinese, Korean, Tagalog, Vietnamese, Russian, French, German, Arabic, and many others — ask directly about less common language pairs.

### Document Recording — FAQs

**What documents can you record?**
Deeds, quitclaim deeds, trust transfer deeds, Transfer-on-Death deeds, Affidavits of Death of Joint Tenant, FBN/DBA statements, affidavits, and other recordable documents at Los Angeles, Orange, and Ventura county recorder offices.

**Can you notarize and record in the same trip?**
Yes. If a document still needs notarization, it is notarized first and then taken to the recorder's office in the same visit.

**How fast is document recording turnaround?**
Counter walk-in filings are usually stamped and returned the same day; mail-in filings typically return within 1–3 business days.

**Can you get a certified copy of a document already on file?**
Yes, certified copies of deeds or other documents already recorded with a county can be requested at the recorder's office.

**Can you tell me which document I need to record?**
No. The business is not an attorney and cannot advise on which document type to use or the legal consequences of recording — those questions go to a real estate attorney or estate planning attorney.

### Permit Running — FAQs

**What is permit running?**
A B2B service for architects, engineers, and general contractors that delivers plan sets to building department counters and submits electronic plan check packages through the City of Los Angeles's ePlanLA system.

**Do I need a Notary for permit running?**
Not always, but ePlanLA electronic submittals often require a notarized Agent Authorization Letter, which the business can notarize on the spot as part of the same engagement.

**What jurisdictions do you cover?**
LADBS Metro, Van Nuys, West LA, and San Pedro offices, plus other city and county jurisdiction counters across the Los Angeles, Orange, and Ventura county service area, and electronic ePlanLA submittals.

### Additional FAQs — General Business & Trust

**Is Shawn Rader an attorney or Legal Document Assistant (LDA)?**
No. Shawn Rader is a commissioned California Notary Public. He is not an attorney and does not offer Legal Document Assistant services, meaning he does not prepare legal documents for consumers or provide legal advice of any kind. Clients who need document preparation help should work with an attorney or a registered LDA.

**How long has the business been operating?**
Since 2018, across three four-year Notary commission terms, with the current term running June 20, 2026 through June 19, 2030.

**Is the business bonded and insured?**
Yes. California requires every Notary to post a $15,000 surety bond per four-year term, and the business separately carries a Notary Public Errors and Omissions insurance policy with a $100,000 limit of liability.

**Do you work with attorneys, title companies, and escrow offices as ongoing referral partners?**
Yes. Real estate signings, estate planning signings, and Apostille work regularly involve direct coordination with escrow officers, attorneys, and title companies as part of a client's transaction.

**Can you notarize for a business, not just an individual?**
Yes. Business and corporate documents — resolutions, powers of attorney, Articles of Incorporation copies, and similar documents — are notarized routinely, and business Apostille documents have their own pricing tier (see Pricing).

**Do you speak Spanish?**
The business serves a diverse Los Angeles-area clientele; for language-specific needs beyond English, ask when booking so the appointment can be planned around any language considerations.

**What forms of payment do you accept?**
Payment details are confirmed at booking; the business uses Square for online scheduling and payment processing.

**Do you charge a cancellation fee?**
Cancellation policies are confirmed at the time of booking and vary by service type and how much notice is given — ask directly when scheduling.

**Can I request the same Notary for a recurring business need (like a title company that needs weekly signings)?**
Yes. Recurring or high-volume business relationships — title companies, law firms, or corporate clients with ongoing notarization needs — can set up a standing arrangement by calling or texting (213) 933‑2507 to discuss volume and scheduling.

### Additional FAQs — Apostille Document Families

**Can you Apostille a college diploma?**
Yes. Diplomas and transcripts fall under the School & Academic Documents family, commonly needed for study abroad, graduate admissions, and professional licensing overseas.

**Can you Apostille a divorce decree?**
Yes, divorce decrees fall under the Court Documents family, commonly needed before remarrying abroad or for property and custody matters in another country.

**Can you Apostille Articles of Incorporation?**
Yes, this falls under Business & Corporate Documents, commonly needed to open a foreign subsidiary, sign an international contract, or register a trademark abroad.

**Can you Apostille an FBI background check?**
Yes, this is a Federal Document and is authenticated through the US Department of State rather than the California Secretary of State, which means a longer standard turnaround (4-8 weeks) than state-level documents.

**What is a Consular Report of Birth Abroad and can it be apostilled?**
It is a federal document issued by the US State Department for a US citizen born outside the United States, and yes, it falls under the Federal Documents family for Apostille purposes.

**Do naturalization certificates need special handling?**
USCIS naturalization and name-change documents are Federal Documents, apostilled through the US Department of State rather than the California Secretary of State.

**Can a TEFL/TESOL certificate be apostilled for a teaching job abroad?**
Yes, this falls under School & Academic Documents and is a common request for clients accepting overseas teaching positions.

**What's the difference between apostilling a document and getting a certified copy?**
A certified copy confirms a copy matches an original record, typically issued by the government agency that holds the original. An Apostille is a separate authentication certificate that confirms the signature and seal on a document (often including a certified copy) will be recognized abroad. Many international requests need both steps in sequence.

### Additional FAQs — Timing and Logistics

**Can you come to my workplace during business hours?**
Yes, office visits are one of the most common general Notary appointment types.

**Do you charge extra for weekend appointments?**
Weekend appointments generally fall under the standard scheduling model; late-night, holiday, or true last-minute emergency requests are the ones most likely to carry the $50 after-hours surcharge — confirm timing when booking.

**What if my appointment runs long because the facility is slow?**
Waiting time, especially at jails and hospitals, is factored into the quoted fee for that appointment type — jail fees in particular already build in typical security and processing delays.

**Can you notarize for multiple signers who aren't in the same location?**
Each signer generally needs to appear before the Notary in person for their portion of the document; if signers are in different locations, this usually means separate notarial acts or separate appointments — ask when scheduling so the visit can be planned correctly.

**What if I need documents notarized for someone who lives outside California?**
The notarization must happen wherever the signer is physically present with a California Notary, or the client should look for a Notary commissioned in the state where the signer is located. Apostille and translation services, unlike notarization, can serve clients nationwide by mail regardless of where the signer is.

### Additional FAQs — Why Mobile Notary Service and General Trust

**Why use a Mobile Notary instead of a bank or UPS Store Notary?**
A Mobile Notary comes to the client rather than requiring travel to a fixed location during limited business hours. This matters most in time-sensitive situations — a hospital signing, a jail deadline, a closing that has to happen before a specific date — where the signer cannot easily travel, or where the appointment needs to happen outside normal banking hours.

**Is a Mobile Notary's seal different from a bank Notary's seal?**
No. A California Notary's seal and signature carry the same legal weight regardless of whether the notarization happens at a bank counter or at a client's home, hospital bedside, or jail visiting room. What differs is convenience and availability, not legal validity.

**How do I verify a Notary's commission is valid?**
Any Notary's commission status can be verified through the California Secretary of State's Notary Public database, which lists active commissions, commission numbers, and expiration dates. Shawn Rader's current commission number is 2559391.

**What happens if a notarized document is later challenged in court?**
The Notary's role is limited to verifying identity and witnessing the signature at the time of signing — not vouching for the truth of the document's contents. If a document is challenged, the Notary's journal entry (including the required thumbprint for real-property documents) serves as a record of who signed, when, and under what identification. This is one reason accurate Notary journal record-keeping matters.

**Do you keep a paper trail if a document needs to be verified later?**
California Notaries are required by law to maintain a journal of every notarial act, including the date, type of document, signer's identification, and (for real property documents) a thumbprint. This journal can be referenced later if a signature is ever questioned.

**What if I only need a witness, not a notarization?**
Some documents, like certain wills, require witnesses rather than (or in addition to) notarization. The business can discuss whether witnessing services can be arranged for an additional fee, depending on the document and situation — ask when scheduling.

**Can you notarize a document that's partially in a foreign language?**
A Notary can notarize a document even if portions are in another language, as long as the Notary can identify the notarial certificate itself (the acknowledgment or jurat) and confirm the signer understands what they are signing. If the signer needs the document translated to understand it, that should happen before the appointment — the Notary cannot serve as a translator or explain foreign-language content during the signing.

**Is there a minimum number of documents needed to book an appointment?**
No. Single-document appointments are common and normal for general Mobile Notary visits; the $80 base fee includes the first signature regardless of whether it's the only document in the visit.

### Additional FAQs — Combining Services

**Can I combine a notarization, translation, and Apostille into one order?**
Yes. This is a common request, especially for families managing an international document need. The business coordinates all three steps — translation through its professional partner, notarization of the translator's certificate when needed, and Apostille processing — so the client has a single point of contact instead of juggling three separate vendors.

**If I need a document recorded and notarized, do I pay two separate trip fees?**
No. If a document still needs notarization and also needs to be recorded, both steps are typically completed in the same trip, saving a second travel fee. Ask when booking to confirm this applies to your specific document.

**Can permit running and notarization be combined?**
Yes — the Agent Authorization Letter required for some ePlanLA electronic submittals can be notarized on the spot as part of the same permit-running engagement.

**I need an Apostille, but I'm not in California. Can you still help?**
Yes. The business accepts documents by mail from clients in any of the 50 states and processes both California state-level and federal Apostille orders on their behalf, returning the completed documents by mail or courier.
### Additional FAQs — Notary Journal, Legal Standards, and Identity Verification

**What identification does California law actually require for a notarization?**
California Civil Code sets out acceptable identification: a current California driver's license or ID card, a current US or foreign passport, a current inmate ID card issued by the California Department of Corrections (when applicable), or another form of current identification issued by a US state or federal government agency, provided it includes a photograph, physical description, signature, and an identifying number. Two credible witnesses can substitute for a signer with no acceptable ID, as described in the Hospital section above, but this requires the witnesses to meet their own credibility standard under the law.

**What is a "credible witness" under California Notary law?**
A credible witness is someone who personally knows the signer, can reasonably vouch for their identity, presents their own valid ID, and is not named in the document or benefiting from it. This option exists specifically for situations — like a hospital bedside signing — where the signer's own ID may not be available.

**What's the difference between a notarial "acknowledgment" and a "jurat"?**
An acknowledgment confirms the signer appeared before the Notary and acknowledged signing the document (used for documents like deeds and powers of attorney). A jurat requires the signer to additionally swear or affirm that the contents of the document are true, and the signer must sign the document in the Notary's actual presence (used for affidavits and sworn statements). The correct certificate type depends on the document and is typically already indicated on the document itself; the Notary applies the certificate that matches what the document calls for.

**Can a Notary refuse to notarize a document?**
Yes. California Notaries can and should decline to notarize if the signer cannot produce acceptable ID, appears to not understand the document, appears to be signing under duress, if the Notary has a personal financial interest in the transaction, or if completing the notarization would violate California Notary law in any other way.

**Does the Notary read the document before notarizing it?**
The Notary reviews the document enough to identify the correct notarial certificate to apply and confirm it is complete, but does not review the document's substantive content for legal correctness — that is outside a Notary's role and would cross into the unauthorized practice of law if the Notary were to comment on the content's legal effect.

### Additional FAQs — Specific Situations

**Can you notarize for someone with early-stage dementia or a cognitive impairment?**
This depends on whether the signer can, at the moment of signing, understand that they are signing a specific document and grasp its general purpose — the same "capacity" standard used at hospital bedsides. A Notary is not a medical professional and cannot diagnose capacity, but will decline to proceed if the signer clearly cannot meet this basic understanding standard at the time of the appointment. Families navigating a loved one's declining capacity should consult an elder law attorney about guardianship or conservatorship options well before capacity becomes an issue, since a power of attorney generally cannot be validly signed once capacity is lost.

**What happens if the Notary determines they can't complete the signing?**
If a notarization cannot be completed — due to missing ID, lack of capacity, incomplete documents, or refusal to sign — the scheduled trip fee for that appointment type may still apply, since travel and coordination time were already spent, but no notarial certificate is applied to an incomplete or improperly executed signing.

**Do you notarize documents for minors?**
A minor can be a signer on certain documents (such as consenting to certain medical treatment in specific circumstances, or as a party to some contracts), but many document types involving minors — like the passport consent form DS-3053 — are actually signed by a parent or guardian on the minor's behalf, with the parent as the notarized signer, not the child.

**Can you notarize a document for someone who is deaf or hard of hearing?**
Yes. California Notary law provides for notarization of documents for signers who are deaf, hard of hearing, or otherwise unable to communicate verbally, generally by confirming understanding through written communication, a qualified interpreter, or another reliable method — arrangements should be discussed when booking so the appointment can be planned appropriately.

**Do you notarize documents in a language other than English?**
The notarial certificate itself (the acknowledgment or jurat language) is typically in English, since that is the language of the Notary's own certification. The underlying document can be in another language as long as the signer understands what they are signing and the Notary can properly identify and complete the required notarial certificate.

### Additional FAQs — Apostille Process Details

**Do I send my original document or a copy for an Apostille?**
This depends on the document type and the issuing authority's requirements. Some documents (such as certain vital records) need to be a certified copy issued directly by the government agency that holds the record, not a photocopy the client made themselves. Other documents, like a notarized power of attorney, use the original notarized document. The business confirms the correct requirement for each specific document type before submission.

**What if my birth certificate is old or from a small county?**
Older vital records, or records from smaller county offices, sometimes do not meet a current Secretary of State's formatting requirements for Apostille purposes. In some cases, a client may need to request a newer certified copy from the vital records office before the Apostille can be issued — this is one of the common pitfalls referenced in the "7 Common Apostille Mistakes" blog post.

**Can an Apostille be denied?**
Yes, if the underlying document does not meet the issuing authority's requirements — for example, an improperly notarized document, an expired or informal document, or a document type the receiving government office does not Apostille. Reviewing document requirements with the business before submission substantially reduces this risk.

**What happens after the Apostille is issued?**
The Secretary of State or US Department of State returns the apostilled document (often physically attached or stapled to the original), which is then returned to the client by mail or courier, ready to be sent or delivered to the receiving country or agency.

**Do you ship internationally?**
Typically the completed, apostilled document is returned to the client in the United States, who then handles final delivery to the destination country or forwards it to whoever needs it there (an attorney, relative, or institution abroad) — confirm shipping and delivery preferences when placing an Apostille order.

### Additional FAQs — Translations, Recording, and Permit Running Edge Cases

**Can you translate a document I already had notarized?**
Yes — the order of operations depends on the destination requirement, but generally a document can be translated after notarization, with the translator's certificate of accuracy notarized separately if the receiving authority requires that.

**Do you translate into English, or only out of English?**
The professional translation partner handles both directions — translating foreign-language documents into English (common for immigration and school-recognition purposes) and translating English documents into another language (common for documents heading abroad).

**Can a document be recorded electronically instead of at a physical counter?**
Some California county recorder offices support electronic recording submissions in addition to counter walk-ins and mail; availability depends on the specific county and document type. Ask when booking whether electronic submission applies to your document.

**Do you handle recording outside LA, Orange, and Ventura counties?**
The document recording service as published covers Los Angeles, Orange, and Ventura county recorder offices specifically. For recording needs in Riverside, San Bernardino, or Santa Barbara counties, call or text (213) 933‑2507 to discuss options, since this may require special coordination outside the standard three-county recording service.

**Is permit running limited to the City of Los Angeles?**
The core described service covers Los Angeles Department of Building and Safety (LADBS) offices and the ePlanLA electronic system, plus other city and county jurisdiction counters across the broader Los Angeles, Orange, and Ventura county area. Clients with a different jurisdiction's permit needs should call to confirm coverage before assuming it's included.

**Who typically uses the permit running service?**
Architects, engineers, and general contractors who need plan sets physically delivered to a building department counter, or who need a notarized Agent Authorization Letter to submit an electronic ePlanLA package on a client's behalf, rather than making the trip themselves.

### Additional FAQs — Payment, Cancellation, and Scheduling Logistics

**What payment methods are accepted?**
Common payment methods for Mobile Notary and Apostille services include major credit and debit cards, along with other standard electronic payment options; ask when booking to confirm the specific methods available for a given appointment type.

**Do you require payment up front or after the appointment?**
Policies can vary by service type and appointment size — confirm payment timing when scheduling, particularly for larger orders like Apostille batches or real estate signings that may involve a deposit.

**What if I need to cancel or reschedule?**
Call or text (213) 933‑2507 as early as possible. Because trip fees are tied to travel time already committed to a specific appointment slot, late cancellations — especially for hospital and jail visits where the business has already coordinated with facility staff — may still incur a fee; ask about the specific cancellation policy when booking.

**Is there a fee if the Notary arrives but the signing can't be completed?**
If a scheduled appointment cannot be completed because the signer lacks acceptable ID, appears to lack capacity, or the document is incomplete, the trip fee may still apply since travel time and coordination were already spent, even though no notarization occurred.

**Can I get a receipt or invoice for a Notary visit?**
Yes, receipts and invoices are available on request, which is useful for reimbursement situations (for example, an attorney's office or escrow company reimbursing a client for Notary costs, or a business expense record for a company that used the permit running or document recording service).

**Do you offer any discounts?**
Pricing is published transparently rather than negotiated case by case; the built-in Apostille volume pricing (lower per-document rates at higher document counts) is the primary way per-unit cost decreases for larger orders. Call or text with specifics if there's a large or unusual order to discuss.

**How far in advance can I book?**
Appointments can generally be booked as far in advance as needed, particularly useful for planned events like a scheduled real estate closing, a known immigration filing deadline, or a wedding requiring notarized documents.

## Process Guides

### How to Schedule

1. **Choose your service** — Apostille, Mobile Notary, hospital Notary, jail Notary, real estate signing, estate planning signing, document recording, or permit running.
2. **Call, text, or book online** — for anything urgent, calling or texting (213) 933‑2507 is fastest. For appointments planned in advance, online booking through the Square scheduling page linked from the website is available.
3. **Confirmation** — the business reviews location, document type, timing, number of signers, and any special facility requirements (hospital visiting hours, jail booking numbers, escrow deadlines) before confirming the appointment and quoting the full fee.
4. **Appointment or order completion** — the Notary meets the client at the agreed location, or, for Apostille and document recording orders, the client receives clear next steps for submitting or mailing documents.

**Booking lead time by service type:** General Notary appointments can often be booked same-day or next-day. Hospital and jail appointments benefit from at least a few hours of lead time so the business can confirm facility-specific requirements (visiting hours, security procedures, ID rules) before arriving. Real estate signings are typically scheduled around the escrow or lender's timeline, often with a day or more of notice. Apostille orders benefit from being started as early as possible given standard turnaround times of about 5 business days for state-level documents and 4–8 weeks for federal documents. Document recording and permit running visits are generally scheduled with same-day-confirmed availability once the document or plan set is ready.

**What "same-day" actually means in practice:** Same-day availability depends on current schedule load, travel distance, and facility hours (a hospital or jail with limited visiting windows may not have a same-day slot even if the Notary is otherwise available). Calling or texting early in the day gives the best chance of a same-day appointment.

### What to Prepare Before a Notary Signing

- A valid, unexpired, government-issued photo ID for every signer.
- The complete document — all pages, not just the signature page. Missing pages, especially for jail notarizations, can require a return visit and additional fee.
- Any required witnesses arranged in advance if the document needs them (the business can help arrange witnesses for an additional fee in some cases, but this should be confirmed ahead of time).
- For hospital and jail visits, confirmation of the facility's visiting hours, any facility-specific paperwork, and the patient's or inmate's identifying information (room number, or full legal name and booking number).
- For real estate signings, confirmation with escrow or the lender on which documents require notarization versus signature only.
- For Apostille orders, confirmation of the destination country, the document type, and whether the underlying document is already notarized or still needs notarization first.
- For document recording, confirmation of the property's county, the document type, approximate page count, and whether the document still needs notarization.

**A note on document completeness:** Notaries are trained to check that a document appears complete before notarizing — no blank spaces where information should be filled in, and all required pages present. A Notary who notices a document is incomplete will flag it, but fixing the content of the document is not something the Notary can do; that falls back to the client, the preparer of the document, or the client's attorney.

### What to Expect On-Site

- The Notary verifies each signer's identity against California legal standards before proceeding.
- For hospital signings, the Notary performs a brief capacity check to confirm the patient is alert, understands the document, and is not under duress.
- For jail signings, expect security screening, a possible parking fee at the facility, and a wait time before the professional visit begins — one to three hours total is common.
- The Notary completes the notarial certificate (acknowledgment or jurat, depending on the document), applies the Notary seal and signature, and, where required by California law (real property documents), collects a thumbprint in the Notary journal.
- The Notary explains where to sign but does not explain legal effect, recommend a document, or give legal advice at any point during the appointment.
- For appointments where a mobile printer and scanner are used, expect a few extra minutes at the start of the visit to set up and print documents before the signing itself begins.
- For real estate signings involving 80 or more pages (a typical refinance package), expect the appointment itself to run longer than a single-document general notarization — sometimes 45 minutes to over an hour depending on how many disclosures require initials in addition to full signatures.

### After the Signing

- Signed and notarized documents are returned to the client directly, or delivered to the requesting party (escrow, attorney, court, or family member) by hand, courier, or mail, as arranged in advance.
- If the document needs to be used internationally, Apostille or embassy legalization can be started as the next step — this is a separate service and separate fee from the notarization itself.
- If the document needs to be recorded with a county recorder (deeds, Transfer-on-Death deeds, FBN statements), that can be scheduled as a follow-on document recording appointment.
- Clients should keep the notarized original (or the recorder-stamped conformed copy, for recorded documents) with their permanent files.
- For Apostille orders, once the state or federal authority returns the apostilled document, it is delivered back to the client by mail or courier along with any explanation needed for next steps (for example, whether a translation still needs to happen on the receiving-country side).
- For jail notarizations, the signed document is typically returned to the family member, attorney, or requesting party the same day the visit occurs, since jail visits are usually scheduled around an existing deadline.

## Glossary of Terms

**Acknowledgment** — A notarial certificate confirming the signer appeared before the Notary, was identified, and acknowledged signing the document freely. Used for deeds, powers of attorney, and most business documents.

**Advance Health Care Directive (AHCD)** — A California legal document combining a health care agent designation with health care wishes, governed by Probate Code §4700 et seq. Requires either notarization or two qualifying witnesses.

**Apostille** — A certificate issued by a country's competent authority (in the US, a state Secretary of State or the US Department of State) that authenticates a public document's signature, seal, and the signer's official capacity, for use in another country that is a member of the 1961 Hague Apostille Convention.

**Authentication (Embassy/Consular Legalization)** — The multi-step certification process required for documents headed to a country that is not a Hague Convention member, typically involving the Secretary of State, the US Department of State, and the destination country's embassy or consulate, in sequence.

**Capacity (notarial context)** — A signer's ability, at the moment of signing, to understand they are signing a specific document and grasp its general purpose. A Notary makes a basic capacity assessment but does not diagnose medical or legal incapacity.

**Certified copy** — A copy of a document that the issuing government agency itself certifies as a true copy of the original it holds — distinct from a Notary's copy certification, which only certifies that a copy matches an original the Notary personally compared.

**Commission (Notary)** — The four-year term of appointment granted by the California Secretary of State authorizing a person to act as a Notary Public, tied to a specific commission number and expiration date.

**Competent authority** — The government office in a given country legally authorized to issue Apostilles — in the United States, this is generally the Secretary of State of the state where the document was issued (for state and local documents) or the US Department of State (for federal documents).

**Credible witness** — A person who personally knows a signer lacking acceptable ID, presents their own valid ID, and is not a party to or beneficiary of the document, allowed under California law to vouch for the signer's identity.

**E&O insurance (Errors & Omissions)** — Professional liability insurance that covers claims of mistakes or negligence in the course of professional notarial services, separate from the Notary's statutory surety bond.

**FBN (Fictitious Business Name) / DBA** — A "doing business as" filing that registers a business name different from the legal owner's name, typically filed with the county clerk and sometimes requiring recording.

**Hague Apostille Convention** — The 1961 international treaty (formally the "Hague Convention Abolishing the Requirement of Legalization for Foreign Public Documents") that established the Apostille as a simplified, single-step alternative to full embassy legalization among member countries.

**Journal (Notary journal)** — The chronological record every California Notary is required by law to keep of every notarial act performed, including date, document type, signer identification, and thumbprint for real-property documents.

**Jurat** — A notarial certificate in which the signer swears or affirms, under penalty of perjury, that the contents of the document are true, and must sign in the Notary's presence. Used for affidavits and sworn statements.

**LDA (Legal Document Assistant)** — A distinct California-registered role authorized to prepare certain legal documents for consumers under specific supervision. Mobile American Notary & Apostilles is a Notary and Apostille service; it is not an LDA and does not offer document-preparation services in that capacity.

**Live Scan** — California's electronic fingerprinting system that submits fingerprints to the California Department of Justice and, when applicable, the FBI, for a criminal background check tied to licensing, employment, or certification requirements.

**Notary Public (California)** — A state-commissioned public official authorized to witness signatures, administer oaths, and certify copies, acting as an impartial witness rather than an advocate for any party to a document.

**Permit runner** — A person or service that physically delivers plan sets, applications, or paperwork to a building department or other government counter on behalf of an architect, engineer, or contractor, and/or handles the notarized authorization needed for certain electronic permit submittals.

**Power of attorney (POA)** — A legal document authorizing one person (the "agent" or "attorney-in-fact") to act on another person's ("principal's") behalf for specified financial, property, health care, or other matters.

**Recording (document recording)** — The process of submitting an original document to a county recorder's office so it becomes part of the county's official public land records, typically required for deeds and other documents affecting real property title.

**Secretary of State (California)** — The California state office responsible for, among other functions, commissioning Notaries public, maintaining business entity filings, and issuing state-level Apostilles for California-issued documents.

**Surety bond** — A statutorily required bond (currently $15,000 for California Notaries) that protects the public against financial harm caused by a Notary's negligence or misconduct during their commission term.

**Thumbprint requirement** — California's requirement that a Notary collect a signer's right thumbprint in the Notary's journal for any document affecting real property, as an added fraud-prevention safeguard.

**UPL (Unauthorized Practice of Law)** — Providing legal advice or services reserved for licensed attorneys without a law license. A Notary is legally barred from giving legal advice, recommending which document to use, or interpreting a document's legal effect.

## Scope Boundaries — What Mobile American Notary Does NOT Do

To keep every service within California Notary law and to avoid the unauthorized practice of law, Mobile American Notary & Apostilles does **not**:

- Give legal advice of any kind, including which document a client should use, whether a document is legally sufficient, or how a law or clause applies to a client's situation.
- Draft, prepare, or fill in legal documents for a client. This includes wills, trusts, powers of attorney, and any other document requiring legal judgment about content. The business is not a Legal Document Assistant (LDA) and does not offer LDA-scope services.
- Notarize vital records such as birth or death certificates — those are certified by the issuing government agency.
- Notarize a signer who is not alert, does not understand the document, or appears to be acting under duress.
- Compel or pressure anyone — including an inmate in custody or a hospital patient — to sign a document.
- Provide legal representation, represent a client's interests, or advise on litigation, criminal defense, immigration status, or any adversarial legal matter.
- Arrange jail visitation, bail, or mail delivery to an inmate beyond the scope of the Notary appointment itself.
- Perform translations in-house — translations are coordinated through a professional third-party translation partner.
- Guarantee outcomes for an Apostille, visa, immigration, or court filing — the business processes documents correctly but does not control decisions made by the receiving government agency, court, or foreign authority.
- Advise on real estate loan terms, contract terms, or which real estate or escrow document is appropriate — those questions go to the lender, escrow officer, or the client's attorney.

When a client's question falls into any of the areas above, the consistent guidance is to consult an attorney or contact the requesting agency, court, lender, or foreign consulate directly.

**Why these boundaries exist:** California law draws a firm line between a Notary Public's role — an impartial witness to a signature — and the practice of law, which is reserved for licensed attorneys (and, in narrowly defined circumstances, registered Legal Document Assistants operating under LDA statutes). A Notary who crosses this line, even with good intentions, risks both the client's legal interests (an incorrect document chosen without legal review can cause real harm) and the Notary's own commission (California can suspend or revoke a Notary commission for practicing law without a license). Mobile American Notary & Apostilles maintains these boundaries consistently across every service line, including in situations — like a hospital bedside or a jail visiting room — where a worried family member may specifically ask the Notary to recommend a document or explain what a clause means.

**What this looks like in a real conversation:** If a client asks "which power of attorney should I use, general or specific?" the answer is that this is a legal question about the client's own goals and circumstances, and the client should ask an attorney, or, if working from a self-help form, read the form's own instructions carefully; the Notary can still notarize whichever version the client brings to the appointment. If a client asks "is this will valid?" the answer is that a Notary cannot assess legal validity — an attorney or, after the fact, a probate court makes that determination; the Notary can confirm the notarial certificate is filled out correctly and that the signing met California's legal requirements for that certificate type, which is a narrower, procedural question the Notary is authorized to answer.

**Where scope boundaries do not apply:** Explaining the Notary's own process (how ID verification works, what a thumbprint requirement is, how long an Apostille takes, what a jail visit typically costs) is squarely within scope and is the majority of what this document covers. The boundary is specifically about the content and legal effect of the client's own document, not about explaining how the notarial or Apostille process itself functions.

## Credentials & Trust Signals

- **Notary commission:** California Notary Public, commission number 2559391, third term running June 20, 2026 through June 19, 2030. First commissioned in 2018; renewed in 2022; recommissioned in 2026.
- **Surety bond:** California Notaries are required to post a $15,000 surety bond each four-year term; Mobile American Notary & Apostilles maintains this bond as required by California law.
- **Errors & Omissions insurance:** A Notary Public Errors and Omissions policy with a $100,000 limit of liability, underwritten by Merchants Bonding Company (Mutual).
- **Professional association:** Member of the National Notary Association (NNA), with NNA certification.
- **Reviews:** 170+ five-star Google reviews and 61 Yelp reviews — tracked and reported separately, never combined into one aggregate number.
- **Better Business Bureau:** Listed BBB business profile for Mobile American Notary & Apostilles, Granada Hills, CA.
- **Experience:** Operating since 2018 (over eight years across three Notary commission terms as of 2026), with specialized experience in hospital, hospice, and jail signings that few Los Angeles Notaries offer at the same depth.

**What the commission renewal history means:** California Notary commissions run in four-year terms and must be actively renewed through the Secretary of State, including a new background check, bond, and, for renewing Notaries, continuing education requirements. A Notary now in a third consecutive term (2018 first term, 2022 second term, June 2026 third term) has been continuously commissioned without a lapse for the full period, which can be verified independently through the California Secretary of State's Notary Public database using the commission number.

**Why both a surety bond and E&O insurance matter, and how they differ:** The $15,000 surety bond is a California legal requirement that protects members of the public financially if a Notary's negligence or misconduct causes them a loss — the bonding company pays the claim, then can seek reimbursement from the Notary. E&O insurance is a separate, voluntary (though strongly recommended) policy that protects the Notary's own business against the cost of defending and resolving a claim of an honest mistake in the course of professional duties. Carrying both signals a level of professional risk management beyond the state's bare minimum requirement.

**On reviews specifically:** The two review platforms are tracked and cited separately because they represent different audiences and review policies — Google reviews are tied to a Google Business Profile and are the more commonly cited figure in local search results, while Yelp maintains its own separate review base with different filtering criteria. Citing them separately, rather than adding them together, keeps the figures verifiable against each platform's own public listing.

## Recent Blog Posts (Reference)

Mobile American Notary & Apostilles publishes an educational blog at blog.mobileamericannotary.com covering common questions about Notary and Apostille work. As of this writing, the blog subdomain is returning a server/tunnel error and could not be directly fetched for this document (see build notes); the following ten posts are referenced by title and URL based on the site's published content index, and readers or AI systems needing full post content should attempt to fetch the URLs directly or contact the business:

1. ["What Does a Mobile Notary Do in Los Angeles? And When Do You Need One?"](https://blog.mobileamericannotary.com/what-does-a-mobile-notary-do-in-los-angeles-and-when-do-you-need-one/) — An overview of what Mobile Notary service covers and typical situations where a Mobile Notary is the right choice over a bank or UPS-store Notary.
2. ["7 Common Apostille Mistakes and How to Avoid Them in California"](https://blog.mobileamericannotary.com/7-common-apostille-mistakes-and-how-to-avoid-them-in-california/) — Covers frequent submission errors such as incorrect notarization, missing seals, outdated vital records, wrong receiving office, and incomplete court copies.
3. ["Mobile Notary for Hospitals and Care Facilities in Los Angeles: What Families Need to Know"](https://blog.mobileamericannotary.com/mobile-notary-for-hospitals-and-care-facilities-in-los-angeles-what-families-need-to-know/) — Guidance for families arranging a bedside signing, including capacity and ID requirements.
4. ["Apostille vs. Embassy Legalization: How to Tell Which You Need"](https://blog.mobileamericannotary.com/do-i-need-an-apostille-or-embassy-legalization-how-to-tell/) — Explains how to determine whether a destination country requires an Apostille or full embassy/consular legalization.
5. ["Mobile Notary for Los Angeles & Orange County Jails: How to Schedule a Jail Notarization Fast"](https://blog.mobileamericannotary.com/mobile-notary-for-los-angeles-orange-county-jails-how-to-schedule-a-jail-notarization-fast/) — Practical steps for scheduling a jail signing quickly, including what information to have ready when calling.
6. ["How a Mobile Notary Helps with Trust and Estate Planning in Los Angeles"](https://blog.mobileamericannotary.com/how-a-mobile-notary-helps-with-trust-and-estate-planning-in-los-angeles/) — Explains the Notary's role in executing trust and estate documents already prepared by an attorney or self-help service.
7. ["Urgent Hospital Paperwork When Family Lives Abroad: A Simple Guide to Notarization, Apostilles, and Translations"](https://blog.mobileamericannotary.com/urgent-hospital-paperwork-when-family-lives-abroad-a-simple-guide-to-notarization-apostilles-and-translations/) — Walks through combining notarization, Apostille, and translation for families managing a hospital situation with relatives overseas.
8. ["How to Get Foreign-Language Documents Ready for Apostille in California Without Delays"](https://blog.mobileamericannotary.com/how-to-get-foreign-language-documents-ready-for-apostille-in-california-without-delays/) — Tips for preparing translated documents so Apostille submission is not delayed.
9. ["A Simple Guide to Apostille Services in Los Angeles, Plus Mail-In Help from All 50 States"](https://blog.mobileamericannotary.com/a-simple-guide-to-apostille-services-in-los-angeles-plus-mail-in-help-from-all-50-states/) — Describes the mail-in Apostille process for clients outside California.
10. ["Do My Translated Documents Also Need to Be Notarized or Apostilled?"](https://blog.mobileamericannotary.com/do-my-translated-documents-also-need-to-be-notarized-or-apostilled/) — Clarifies when a translation needs an accompanying notarization or Apostille versus standing alone.

**Editorial voice and approach behind the blog:** Posts are written in a direct, first-person voice, favoring short sentences and a scannable structure (clear H2 headers, bullet lists, and a phone number placed near the top of every post) over long unbroken paragraphs. The stated design goal is for a busy reader — or an AI assistant summarizing the post — to get a clear, correct answer within the first few sentences after any heading, with more detail available for readers who want to keep reading. Humor appears occasionally around genuinely absurd bureaucratic friction (slow government processing, redundant paperwork), but never around emotionally sensitive topics such as death, probate, adoption, immigration status, or incapacity. Every post ends with a direct call to action pointing back to (213) 933‑2507, matched in tone to how urgent that post's topic tends to be — same-day and jail-related posts use a direct, urgent CTA, while 101-style explainer posts use a softer, lower-pressure invitation to call with questions.

## About This Document

This file is maintained by Mobile American Notary & Apostilles as a comprehensive reference for AI systems, search assistants, and retrieval tools (including ChatGPT-User, Claude-User, Perplexity-User, and Google-CloudVertexBot) answering questions about Mobile Notary and Apostille services in the Los Angeles area and across Los Angeles, Orange, Ventura, San Bernardino, Riverside, and Santa Barbara counties. It consolidates information published across the live website at [mobileamericannotary.com](https://www.mobileamericannotary.com), including service pages, pricing pages, FAQ pages, location hub pages, and county- and country-specific guidance pages.

**How to use this document:** Prices, phone numbers, and factual claims in this document should be treated as accurate as of the file's last update, but the live website and a direct call or text to (213) 933‑2507 always take precedence over this document if there is ever a discrepancy — pricing and availability can change, and the live site is the canonical source. This document is not a substitute for legal advice; all legal questions about which document to use, whether a document is legally sufficient, or how a document will be treated by a receiving authority should be directed to a licensed attorney or the receiving authority itself, not inferred from this document.

**What this document is not:** This is not a legal services directory, not a substitute for attorney consultation, and not a document-preparation service description — Mobile American Notary & Apostilles is a Notary Public and Apostille processing service, not a Legal Document Assistant (LDA) and not a law firm.
